California
Fair Political
Practices Commission
october 9, 1990
Iris P. Yang
ucDonough, HoIland and Al len
Attorneys at Law
555 capitol uall, suite 950
Sacramento, CA 9 5814
Re:
Under
represent
conni ss ion
Dear Us. Yang:
This letter is in response to your request for advice regard-
inq the conf I ict-of- intereit provisions of the Political 
Reform
;;i 
iah"-;Acii) 1 ana confirms- the terephone advice provided to you
on September 18, 1990.
OUESTION
the provisions of the Act, Day a county supervisor
the iirterests of a client lefoie the county planning
and the county board of supervisors?
CONCLUSION
Under the provisions of the Act, a county supervisor may
represent the i-nterests of a client before the county plannlng
coiuission and before the county board of supervisors when.the
client is a corporation wholly owned by the supervj'sor's nlnor
children.
FACTS
Sierra County Supervisor Craig UcHenry has established an
irrevocable fanil| trust, the sole beneficiaries of which are his
ifri"" nirror chil.dien. The trust was funded by supervisor McHenry

ANALYSIS
section 87■00 prohibits a pt
il:::::i:I:::呈::::]li::: :ifi::il
financial intereSt.  A member Of
a pub■iC offiCial.  (SectiOn 32048.)

from the effeCt On the pub■ic general■y′  on:
^J
(a) Any business entity in r'rhich the public.
orriciai has a direct or indlrect investment worth
one thousand dollars (S1,000) or Dore'
2 Th"t" facts include
telephone conversation
infornation you provided to ne during our
of Septenber 7, 1990.


File
Page
A-90-536
(b) Any real property in r.rhich the Public
officiaL has a direct or indirect interest worth
one thousand dollars (51,0oo) or lnore,
(c) Any source of income, other than gifts
and other than loans by a commercial lending
institution 
in the regular course of business on
terms available to the public without regard to
official 
status, aggregating tlro hundred fifty doI-
Iars (5250) or more in value provided to, received
by or pronised to the public official within 12
months prior to the time vhen the decision is nade.
(d) Any business entity in which the public
official 
is a director, officer, partner, trustee,
employee, or holds any position of management.
(e) Any donor of, or any intermedj.ary or
agent for a donor of, a gift or gifts aggregating
two hundred fifty dollars ($250) or more in value
provided to, received by, or pronised to the public
official 
within 12 months prior to the tine when
the decision is rnade.
For purposes of this section, indirect invest-
nent or interest neans any investnent or interest
owned by the spouse or dependent child of a public
official, 
by an agent on behatf of a public of-
ficial, 
or by a business entity or trust in which
the official, 
the official's 
agents, spouse, and
dependent chiLdren own directly, indirectly, or
beneficially a 1O-percent interest or greater.
section 87103 .
Your facts indicate that Supervisor McHenry has established
an irrevocable fanily trust for the benefit of his three ninor
children. 
This trust holds the stock of a corporation which orvns
certain real property within Sierra county. Supervisor HcHenry
has not received any income fron the trust or fron the corPoration
in the twelve months preceding the decision. However, pursuant to
the provisions of Section 87Lo3, Supervisor McHenry has an
indirect i.nvestment or interest in the trust established for the
benefit of his dependent children. 
For purposes of our analysis
lre assume that Supervisor McHenry has a direct or indirect invest-
ulent worth in excess of $1,ooo in the corporation. sinilarly, 
for
purposes of our analysis, we assune that Supervisor !.tcHenry has a
direct or indirect interest vroEth nore than 51,000 in the real
property owned by the corporation. Thus, Supervisor !,IcHenry nay
^li6t atte:npt to use his official 
position to influence a decision
which will have a foreseeable and naterial financial effect on the
corporation in which he has an indirect investnent or interest.
No.
3


File No. A-90-53 6
Page 4
Prohibited attenpts to influence a decision include contact-
ing any member, officer, 
ernployee of the official's 
agency, or any
agency under the appointive or budgetary control of the official's
agency. Thus, if Supervisor McHenry's client LrouId be foreseeably
and naterially affected by the pending decision, the supervisor
may not represent the clj.ent before the county planning commission
which is subject to the appointive and budgetary control of the
county board of supervisors. Additionally, supervisor McHenry may
not represent his client before his ohrn agency, the board of
suPervisors.
Furthermore, it is foreseeable that !.tr. Hood, lrho has been a
source of incorne to supervisor l,tcHenry in excess of S250 in the 12
nonths preceding the decision, will be rnaterially affected by the
decision. This is so because it is foreseeable that, if the
perurit is granted, Mr. Hood will lease the property from the
corporation. Thus, Supervisor UcHenry nay not participate in the
decision.
You correctly refer our attention to Regulation 1870o.1 which
carves out exceptions to the general rule. 
This regulation states
in pertinent part as follows:
(a) with regard to a governruental decision
which is within or before an official's 
aqency or
an agency appointed by or subject to the budgetary
control of his or her aqency, the official 
is at-
tenpting to use his or her official position to
influence the decision if, for the purpose of
influencing the decision, the official 
contacts, or
appears before, or otheruise atteDpts to influence,
any nenber, officer, 
employee or consultant of the
agency. Attenpts to influence include, but are not
linited to, appearances or contacts by the official
on behalf of a business entity, client, or
custoDer .
(b) Notwithstanding subsection (a) an of-
ficiaL is not atternpting to use his or her official
position to infLuence a lrovernnental decision of an
agency covered by subsection (a) if the official:
(1) Appears in the same nanner as any
other nenber of the general public before an
agency in the course of its prescribed
governmental function solely to represent
hinself or herself on a natter which is
related to his or her personal interests. 
An
official,s 
trpersonal interestsrr include, but
(A) An interest in real. property
which is wholly orrned by the official 
or
nenbers of his or her inroediate family.


Fi■e No. A-90-536
Page 5
(B) A business entity whol1y owned
by the official 
or nembers of his or her
immediate fami Iy.
Regulation 18700. 1 (a-) and (b) .
Pursuant to subdivision (b) of Regulation 18700.1, the
general rule does not apply r.rhen a public official 
appears before
his or her agency solelv to represent his or her personal
interests, including interests in real property or a business
wholl-v owned bv the official 
or nembers of his or her inmediate
faruil|.3 
ettnSugrr supervisor l,tcHenry has divested hiroself of alI
interest in the trust, his dependent children are the sole
beneficiaries of the trust and hold an equitable interest in the
corporation and the real property held by the trust. 
(Reaqh v.
KeIlev (1970) 89 Ca1. Rptr. 425, 10 Ca1. App. 3d 1082.)
ihus, Supervisor UcHenry Inay appear before the county planning
connission and the board of supervisors to represent the interests
of the corporation in its application for a conditional use
pertrit. 
wL suggest that supervisor McHenry clarify that he is not
ippearing in his capacity as a county suPervisor. The fact that
it is foreseeable that l,rr. Edward Hood, who is a client and source
of incoue to suPervisor UcHenry, will lease the land if the
conditional use pernit is granted does not alter our conclusion.
We trust this letter adequately responds to your inquiry'
Should you have any further questions regarding this natter, do
not hesitate to call ne at (916) 322-5901.
S incerely ,
scott Hallabrin
Acting General counsel
